title: Distributed Transactions
summary: Two-phase commit (2PC), three-phase commit (3PC), Saga pattern for microservices, and compensating transactions.

sections.content:
## The Problem: Transactions Across Services

In a monolith, a single DB transaction keeps things consistent. In microservices, an operation may span multiple services with separate databases.

```
Place Order:
1. Order Service: create order record
2. Inventory Service: reserve items
3. Payment Service: charge card

If Payment fails after Inventory reserved — you must undo the reservation!
You cannot use a single DB transaction across three services.
```

## Two-Phase Commit (2PC)

A coordinator orchestrates all participants in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Prepare:**
- Coordinator sends `PREPARE` to all participants
- Each participant locks resources, writes to redo log, responds `YES` or `NO`

**Phase 2 — Commit/Abort:**
- If ALL replied YES → coordinator sends `COMMIT` to all
- If ANY replied NO → coordinator sends `ABORT` to all

sections.title: Two-Phase Commit (2PC)
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Coordinator
    participant P1 as Order Service
    participant P2 as Inventory Service
    participant P3 as Payment Service
    Note over C: Phase 1 — PREPARE
    C->>P1: PREPARE
    C->>P2: PREPARE
    C->>P3: PREPARE
    P1-->>C: YES (locked)
    P2-->>C: YES (locked)
    P3-->>C: YES (locked)
    Note over C: All YES → Phase 2 — COMMIT
    C->>P1: COMMIT
    C->>P2: COMMIT
    C->>P3: COMMIT
    P1-->>C: ACK
    P2-->>C: ACK
    P3-->>C: ACK

sections.title: 2PC Problems
sections.content: 2PC is blocking: if the coordinator crashes after sending PREPARE but before sending COMMIT, all participants are stuck holding locks indefinitely. Also, it's synchronous — all participants must be available simultaneously, reducing availability. For these reasons, 2PC is rarely used in modern microservices.

## Saga Pattern

The Saga pattern breaks a long-running transaction into a sequence of local transactions. Each step publishes an event/message that triggers the next step. If a step fails, **compensating transactions** undo previous steps.

### Two implementation styles:

**Choreography** — each service publishes events and listens for others; no central coordinator
**Orchestration** — a central Saga orchestrator tells each service what to do

sections.title: Saga Choreography — Order Placement
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant OS as Order Service
    participant IS as Inventory Service
    participant PS as Payment Service
    participant ES as Email Service
    OS->>OS: Create Order (PENDING)
    OS->>IS: OrderCreated event
    IS->>IS: Reserve Inventory
    IS->>PS: InventoryReserved event
    PS->>PS: Charge Payment
    PS->>ES: PaymentCompleted event
    ES->>ES: Send confirmation
    ES->>OS: OrderConfirmed event
    OS->>OS: Update Order (CONFIRMED)
    Note over PS: If charge fails:
    PS->>IS: PaymentFailed event
    IS->>IS: Release Inventory (compensate)

sections.title: Saga Orchestration
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Orch as Saga Orchestrator
    participant OS as Order Service
    participant IS as Inventory Service
    participant PS as Payment Service
    Orch->>OS: createOrder
    OS-->>Orch: orderCreated
    Orch->>IS: reserveInventory
    IS-->>Orch: inventoryReserved
    Orch->>PS: chargePayment
    PS-->>Orch: paymentFailed!
    Orch->>IS: cancelReservation (compensate)
    IS-->>Orch: reservationCancelled
    Orch->>OS: cancelOrder (compensate)
    OS-->>Orch: orderCancelled

sections.question: What is a compensating transaction in the Saga pattern?
options: A transaction that compensates for slow performance
An action that undoes a previously completed step when a later step fails
A read-only transaction that verifies data consistency
A retry mechanism for failed transactions
sections.explanation: In a Saga, if step N fails, the system must undo all previously completed steps (1 through N-1) using compensating transactions. For example, if payment fails after inventory was reserved, the compensating transaction releases the inventory reservation. Compensating transactions must be idempotent.
